coredata.dc:title: Exploring the excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots for complementary sensing of uranium
coredata.dc:creator: Guin, Saurav K.
Ambolikar, Arvind S.
Paul Guin, Jhimli
Neogy, Suman
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possessing excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties have been exposed here, for the first time, to develop the low cost, non-toxic, metal-free, sustainable real-life adaptable sensors for strategically important and environmentally concerned metals such as uranium(U). The Förster resonance energy transfer involving f-orbitals of U as well as the nonspecific electrostatic association between GQDs and ions of U quench the photoluminescence of the GQDs resulting into photophysical detection of U in a pure solution down to 0.56 μg L−1. The electrochemically reduced GQDs are also found to be more electrocatalytically active than gold and it could detect U down to 2 μg L−1 in aqueous solution having high ionic strengths. This complementary dual-purpose sensor is applicable to determine U in pure solution as well as in the presence of other metal ions in the ground water. Further, we have re-evaluated the kinetic models viz. Nicholson and Klingler-Kochi equations from their respective roots and unambiguously showed neither of the equations is suitable for universal kinetic analysis in electrochemistry. We have persuasively proposed a universal electrodic equation eliminating the perplexities of the traditional kinetic equations. This article paves the unconventional way for environmentally clean synthesis of GQDs possessing excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties expected from the complementary sensor.

head.ce:title: Exploring the excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties of graphene quantum dots for complementary sensing of uranium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai, 400094, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Metallurgy Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai, 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exhibiting the dual excellences of graphene quantum dot (GQD), prepared by an unconventional chemical-free simultaneous oxido-reductive method, in photophysical energy transfer and electrocatalytic reactions for determination of uranium at the ultra-trace levels.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possessing excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties have been exposed here, for the first time, to develop the low cost, non-toxic, metal-free, sustainable real-life adaptable sensors for strategically important and environmentally concerned metals such as uranium(U). The Förster resonance energy transfer involving f-orbitals of U as well as the nonspecific electrostatic association between GQDs and ions of U quench the photoluminescence of the GQDs resulting into photophysical detection of U in a pure solution down to 0.56 μg L−1. The electrochemically reduced GQDs are also found to be more electrocatalytically active than gold and it could detect U down to 2 μg L−1 in aqueous solution having high ionic strengths. This complementary dual-purpose sensor is applicable to determine U in pure solution as well as in the presence of other metal ions in the ground water. Further, we have re-evaluated the kinetic models viz. Nicholson and Klingler-Kochi equations from their respective roots and unambiguously showed neither of the equations is suitable for universal kinetic analysis in electrochemistry. We have persuasively proposed a universal electrodic equation eliminating the perplexities of the traditional kinetic equations. This article paves the unconventional way for environmentally clean synthesis of GQDs possessing excellent photophysical and electrochemical properties expected from the complementary sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: The monolayer disks of graphene oxide having lateral diameters 2–15 nm are known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or carbon dots or carbon quantum dots. The GQDs are one of the trending smart materials possessing prospective futures in the fields of photovoltaic devices, light-emitting diodes, batteries, photocatalysis, bio-imaging, chemical and biological sensors etc [1–12]. By virtue of the stable photoluminescence property, low toxicity, excellent aqueous dispersibility, good biocompatibility and low production-cost, GQDs are widely used as optical sensors for various metal ions and biologically relevant compounds [13–26]. Because of the diversity in the proposed photoluminescence mechanisms of GQDs, the photophysical properties of GQDs are being widely explored worldwide [27,28]. However, the poor electrochemical performance [29] has restricted the applications of GQDs in the field of electrocatalysis and electrochemical sensors except few independent studies [30–36].

Uranium (U) is the ubiquitous and primordial actinide present in the environment especially in soils, rocks and groundwater in various chemical forms. Although, the average concentration of U in the earth’s crust is 4 mg kg−1; U is present in soils and ground water in the concentration ranges 11.7–300 mg kg−1 and 0.1–10 μg L−1 (ppb), respectively [37,38]. The specific activity (Asp) of natural U is about 25 kBq g−1 due to the existence of three radioactive isotopes viz. 238U (99.27%, t1/2 = 4.5 × 109 y; Asp = 12.5 kBq g−1), 235U (0.72%, t1/2 = 7.0 × 108 y; Asp = 80.0 kBq g−1) and 234U (0.005%, t1/2 = 2.5 × 105 y; Asp = 0.2 GBq g−1). That’s why U is one of the elements of great commercial interest in the nuclear energy sectors. However, its alpha toxicity to various body organs such as kidney, brain, liver, heart is of major health concern. Although, according to the WHO’s guidelines for the human health, the concentration of U in the drinking water must be lesser than 30 μg L−1 [39], elevated concentrations of U in the drinking water have been reported in many places in the world including India due to the some geogenic origins [40–44]. Therefore, sensitive determination of U in pure nuclear fuel samples as well as in the drinking water has both technological and social importances and in this direction, only N-doped carbon dots have been applied to determine U in pure water at minimum concentration 0.71 mg L−1 (ppm) [45].

In this article, we have presented basically four major breakthroughs involving GQDs covering the material science, photophysics, electrochemistry and analytical chemistry. Firstly, in this article, we have followed an unconventional route to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at room temperature through a chemically clean, single-step, simultaneous oxido-reductive, non-contact gamma-ray irradiation process, which has never been attempted, except our group, in this domain of research. This synthesis procedure differs far from the conventional top-down routes to prepare GQDs through exfoliation of bulk sp2 carbon materials such as graphene (GN) or graphene oxide (GNO) by acidic oxidation, hydrothermal, solvothermal, electrochemical, chemical exfoliation, microwave-aided, sonication, photo-fenton and ozonolysis methods, which mostly demand toxic chemicals, high temperature or high electricity [46–51]. Those conventional synthesis routes involve high concentrations of inorganic salts, acids and generate many undesired byproducts. Hence, stringent and fussy purification processes such as dialysis and ultra-filtration are often become mandatory to obtain chemically pure form of GQDs in the conventional routes. Further, all the conventional procedures involve only oxidative cutting of GNO, and hence, the final product becomes electrochemically semiconducting or insulating in nature. Therefore, an additional reduction step has to be included for preparing reduced and conducting form of GQDs (rGQDs) suitable for the electrochemical devices. Eliminating all those difficulties, we are the first to propose and design this unconventional chemically clean, single-step, oxido-reductive synthesis route to prepare chemically pure GQDs at room temperature for efficient photophysical and electrochemical dual applications [52]. The dual excellences of GQDs are expected to be suitable for developing low cost, non-toxic complimentary dual-purpose sensors for U, where U on the one hand, has been determined down to sub-ppb levels by monitoring the photoluminescence quenching of the homogeneous aqueous dispersion of GQDs in the presence of U and on the other hand, the casted and electrochemically reduced GQDs (ERGQDs) modified working electrode have enhanced the sensitivity for the determination of U down to few ppb levels by ERGQD’s heterogeneous catalytic interactions in the electrodics of U(VI)/U(V) redox reaction in the saturated Na2CO3 solution.

Secondly, in the due course of this study, we have completely characterized this new product (GQDs), and its photophysical properties have been studied both in the absence and the presence of U to unfurl the photophysical energy transfer mechanism between GQDs and U, which is indeed an f-block element. Here, we are the first to show that other than the nonspecific electrostatic association of the negatively charged oxygenated functional groups of GQDs with the positively charged U ions, the resonance energy transfer from the GQDs to the molecular orbitals of the uranium species is also responsible for the quenching of the photoluminescence of the GQDs in the presence of U, although the later has very minor contribution in the overall quenching mechanism. This phenomenon has helped us to develop an ultra-trace sensor for U in the front-end of the nuclear technology.

Thirdly, we have demonstrated that the ERGQDs, which are basically non-toxic and cheap material, prepared through our non-conventional synthesis route followed by electrochemical reduction are more catalytically active material compared to noble metals such as gold for the heterogeneous electron transfer reactions of U(VI)/U(V) redox couple in water having high ionic strength. In fact, it complements the ultra-trace determination of uranium in the aqueous solutions having high ionic strengths and interfering ions, where photoluminescence based technique is believed to be not suitable. This complimentary utilization of the GQDs (especially bi-parted from a same material prepared in a single synthesis) for the dual-purpose sensor applications is rare in the topic published till date.

It is well known that, the analysis of electrochemical kinetics is a necessary step for studying the electrodics in electrocalalysis, electrochemical sensors, fuel cells, photoelectrochemistry etc., where the traditional equations of either Nicholson [53] or Klingler-Kochi [54] are still being used for analysing electrochemical kinetics from the data of peak-to-peak separations observed in the cyclic voltammograms. The assumptions and limitations of the models are hardly addressed during the kinetic analysis and thus perplexities exist to apply the suitable equation for the data analysis. In this article, we have re-evaluated both the kinetic equations from their respective roots and have demonstrated that neither of the equations is suitable for universal kinetic analysis in electrochemistry. Thus, without making any further delay, we have persuasively made a breakthrough in presenting a universal electrodic emperical equation, which can eliminate the perplexities in choosing the traditional procedure for electrokinetic analysis.

Hence, we believe that the results presented in this manuscript covering the material science, photophysics, electrochemistry and analytical chemistry provide new and important information in the contemporary multidisciplinary researches in GQDs. Further, this article would pave the unconventional, room-temperature, oxido-reductive, easy and environmentally clean synthesis of GQDs possessing both photochemical and electrochemical excellences expected from a sustainable real-life adaptable sensor in both nuclear industries and environmental applications.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium nitrate (KNO3), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), hexaammineruthenium(III) chloride ([Ru(NH3)6]Cl3), uranylnitrate hexahydrate (UO2(NO3)2. 6H2O), strontium chloride hexahydrate (SrCl2. 6H2O), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), aluminium sulfate (Al2(SO4)3), ferric chloride (FeCl3) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) of analytical grades are used as received. The graphene oxide (GNO), purchased from M/s. Global Nanotech with the product certificates, is used as received. Ultrapure Milli-Q Millipore water (18.2 MΩ cm) is used to prepare the solutions or aqueous dispersions.

The GQDs are prepared by following the procedure developed by us [52], but with some optimized modifications to improve the properties. In our earlier study, [52] we found that the photophysical and electrochemical properties of GQDs (having corrected C/O ration = 2.7) were mostly guided by the hydroxyl units and π-electronic structures of GQDs. Therefore, to elevate the photophysical and electrochemical properties of GQDs as per our present requirement, we have plan to increase the concentrations of hydroxyl radicals (OH) and nascent hydrogen (H), which are the primary reacting radicals generated insitu by gamma radiolysis of water having a homogeneous dispersion of GNO. The related reaction mechanisms are available in Section S1 of the Supporting Information File of our previous article [52]. The concentrations of OH and H are increased with the total absorbed dose. A high dose rate would restrict the total synthesis time to a reasonable lab time frame, but it would rapidly deplete the dissolved oxygen involved in the reaction. Thus, here we have optimized the total absorbed dose at relatively high dose rate (11.75 kGy h−1 compared to 1.0 kGy h−1 used in our previous study) to prepare GQDs having high oxygen content (C/O ration = 1.4) suitable for developing low cost, non-toxic, complimentary dual-purpose sensors for U. In brief, 25 mL of 0.5 g L−1 aqueous dispersion of GNO containing 25% H2O2 is irradiated in air for 270 kGy with a dose rate of 11.75 kGy h−1 by using a gamma chamber 5000 (GC-5000) having 60Co gamma source supplied by M/s BRIT, India. The GQDs are separated from H2O2 by rota-evaporation with water for 3 cycles. The solid GQDs are re-dispersed in water and the final concentration of GQDs in water is calculated as 0.83 g L−1. The quantum yield of the dispersion is calculated as 10.2% with respect to rhodamine 6G dye molecule [52]. The GQDs are reproducible as long as the experimental parameters are fixed.

CARL ZEISS LIBRA 200 FE transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV is used to visualize the GQDs at the nanometer and sub-nanometer (high resolution) scales. The IR spectrum is recorded by Alpha table top FTIR spectrophotometer in the wave number range 500–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum is averaged over 36 scans. Seki’s STR300 Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm DPSS laser excitation source is used to study the Raman spectrum. Mg Kα (E = 1253.6 eV) X-ray (SPECS system) source and PHOIBOS hemispherical channel analyzer are used for the x-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements. The GQDs are dried in air on a smooth mica surface and the topography is recorded in air by employing Nanosurf Easyscan-2 atomic force microscope (AFM) instrument operating with Silicon tip (CONTR-10) in contact static mode. The recorded AFM images are studied by the inbuilt programs of the Nanosurf Report 4.1 software. The photophysical properties have been studied by using U-2800 spectrophotometer, Horiba PTI QM 400 spectrofluorometer and time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup having the diode laser of wavelength 339 nm. EQ781 UV cabinet with UV filter is used to visualize the colour of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs in visible and near UV lights. The elemental composition of the ground water is analysed by using an ICP quadrupole MS (PQ2 plus, V.G., UK) after centrifuging the water with an ultracentrifuge (Sigma, model no. 3K30).

CHI–450 B and Autolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical workstations are used to perform the electrochemical experiments. A 10 mL electrochemical cell consisting of a working electrode (Au, GC or ERGQDs), a platinum counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) reference electrode is used in the electrochemical studies at room temperature (T = 298 K). All the potentials reported in this manuscript are measured with respect to this reference electrode. The dissolved oxygen is removed by purging the solution with high purity nitrogen. The Au and GC electrodes are polished using slurry of alumina powder and washed thoroughly with water. 40 μL of aqueous dispersion of GQDs is drop-casted on Au electrode and dried under IR light. The GQDs are electrochemically reduced to ERGQDs by cyclic voltammetry in sat. Na2CO3 at a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 for a single cycle in the potential window 0.1 V to −2.0 V. The actual area (at atomic resolution) of ERGQDs covered on Au is difficult to determine at the present case, as ERGQDs casted on Au is used for the electrochemical experiments. Therefore, the gross electrochemical areas of Au and ERGQDs are calculated as 0.0264 and 0.0266 cm2, respectively, by using the Randles-Sevcik equation from the slopes of the cathodic peak current versus the square root of the scan rate (ranging from 0.025–0.300 V s−1) in the cyclic voltammetry experiments with 5 mM [Ru(NH3)6]3+ in 0.1 M KCl. The number of electrons transferred and the diffusion coefficient of [Ru(NH3)6]3+ in 0.1 M KCl are taken as 1 and 7.1 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, respectively. The current density (j) is calculated by dividing the experimentally recorded current with the calculated electrochemical area.

The synthesized GQDs are circular disks having lateral diameters 4.6 ± 1.1 nm (calculated statistically from the TEM images of 357 GQDs) (Fig. 1
[A]). The lateral diameters of the largest and smallest GQD are found to be 8.8 and 2.3 nm, respectively and most of the GQDs have the lateral diameters in the range 3.5–4.0 nm (Fig. 1 [B]). Although few GQDs have lateral diameters smaller than 3.0 nm, but the population of GQDs of lateral diameters in the range 4.0–6.5 nm is higher. This is a noticeable characteristic observed in the top-down synthesis approach of GQDs. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the electron diffraction from {1100} planes of GQDs along the [0001] zone axis shows hexagonal lattice fringes (inset of Fig. 1 [C]). The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1 [C]) shows that the average lattice spacing between the (
1 ¯
100) planes of GQDs is 0.27 nm corresponding to the lattice constant 3.1 Å of the hexagonal honey-comb type lattice and it is higher compared to the same of normal graphite (2.5 Å). The AFM topography (Fig. 1 [D]) shows the GQDs dispersed on the mica foil are nearly spherical. Although majority of GQDs is isolated islands, but a couple of spherical agglomerates is also formed during the evaporation of water (dispersion medium of GQDs) from the surface of mica foil. The line profile (Fig. 1 [E]) and the 3-dimensional profile (Fig. 1 [F]) of the GQDs show that the average height of isolated GQDs is about 2.5 nm, while the average height of the agglomerates is about 5.5 nm [15]. Therefore, it can be understood from the ex-situ HRTEM and AFM results that the GQDs dispersed on mica foil and TEM grid have multiple layers of graphene as similarly observed by Ghosh et al. [55] However, it is an open science to experimentally confirm whether those multi-layers of GQDs, as observed in the ex-situ AFM and HRTEM experiments, are either the true representation of the unique structure of GQDs dispersed in water or the aggregations formed during the evaporation of water in the drying process.

The FTIR spectrum as shown in Fig. 2
[A] exhibits the characteristic peaks for (COC)Stretching & (CO)Deformation of oxirane unit at 878 & 969 cm−1; stretching of ether or alkoxy linkage at 1017 & 1074 cm−1; (CO)Stretching of aliphatic alcohol, epoxy or phenol & carboxyl at 1123, 1259 & 1384 cm−1; (COC)Stretching of epoxy, (CC)Stretching in alkenes, (CO)Stretching in carbonyls, aldehydes and acids, (CH)Stretching in aldehyde, (CH)Stretching in aliphatic & aromatic units and (OH)Stretching in alcohols at 1460, 1639, 1733, 2854, 2925 & 2958 and 3429 cm−1, respectively [15,16]. The pair of peaks at 2344 & 2362 cm−1 comes from the atmospheric CO2. Therefore, the synthesized GQDs have varieties of oxygenated functional groups as well as sp2/sp3 carbons, which are also evident from the Raman spectrum covering the wave numbers 1000–1800 cm−1 (Fig. 2 [B]). The sharp scattering peaks at 1059 and 1126 cm−1 represent the symmetric stretching of the COC epoxy group and rocking vibration of the methyl group, respectively [56]. The stretching of sp2-sp3 bonded carbons at the edges of GQDs and COOH or phenolic-OH functional groups of GQD produce a small scattering peak at 1167 cm−1 and a sharp Raman peak at 1292 cm−1, respectively [57]. The peak centered at 1570 cm−1 is broadened due to the overlap of the nearby defect activated band D’ (∼1500 cm−1) with the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon (E2g mode) [58]. The intensity of the D-band centered around 1365 cm−1 is sensitive to the nature of the edge states of GQDs in such a way that it is prominent for the armchair edges, but it almost vanishes for the zig-zag edge states. The prominent presence of the D-band in the synthesized GQDs confirms the predominance of arm-chair edges in our GQDs. The pair of scattering peaks at 1414 and 1435 cm−1 represents the symmetric stretching vibrations of the CO or CO at the edges of GQDs [57]. The broad peak centered at 1460 cm−1 represents the symmetric in plane deformation of the methyl group of the GQDs.

The x-ray photoelectron spectra add more insights into the chemical characteristic of the GQDs (Fig. 2 [C & D]). The C/O ration of GQDs is calculated as 1.4, which suggests highly oxygenated nature of the synthesised GQDs. The binding energy profile of C1s peak centered at 284.8 eV can be de-convoluted into three Gaussian peaks centered at 284.3 eV (68.5%), 286.4 eV (4.5%) and 287.9 eV (27.0%) (Fig. 2 [C]). Therefore the major constitutional components of GQDs are sp2 carbons of the graphene backbone and carbonyl (CO) type groups of acids, ketone and aldehyde functional groups. A small proportion of ether or alkoxy (C-O) carbons are also evident in GQDs. The binding energy profile of O1s peak centered at 532.8 eV can be de-convoluted into two Gaussian peaks centered at 532.7 eV (97.9%) and 535.1 eV (2.1%) (Fig. 2 [D]). It reveals the predominance of carbonyl (CO) or epoxy (COC) oxygen atoms over the carboxylic type oxygen atoms (OCO) in the prepared GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-physical characterization of GQDs and homogeneous determination of U at ultra-trace levels
ce:section.ce:para:
The aqueous dispersion of GQDs at pH 5.4 shows strong absorption in the UV region (200–300 nm) originating from the π → π* transition of the aromatic rings (Fig. 3
[A]) [6]. The edge of absorbance is evident in the near-UV region covering the wavelengths 300–400 nm (highlighted by the yellow colour in Fig. 3 [A]). The intercept of the extrapolated (purple arrow) absorption edge to the x-axis is used to estimate the optical band gap of the prepared GQDs as 3.68 eV [59]. The absorption edge of GQDs is commonly assigned to the n → π* transition in the oxygenated functional groups. [8] However, as per the predictions from time-dependent density functional theory [60] and experimental evidences in the photo-physical measurements [61], it can be mentioned that the intra-layer transitions of electrons from the bonding π orbitals to anti-bonding π* orbitals between different molecules or fragments in the same GQD layer contribute to the band edge absorbance. The defect states in GQDs commutatively create an absorption band tail in the wavelength range 400–900 nm [62]. The digital image shows that the aqueous pale yellow dispersion of GQDs turns into green luminescent material on the exposure of near UV lights (inset of Fig. 3 [A]).

The aqueous dispersion of GQDs (pH 5.4) shows a broad photoluminescence (PL) peak centered at 456.1 nm on excitation at 345 nm. The PL peak could be de-convoluted into two PL sub-peaks centered at 451.5 nm (2.75 eV) and 510.3 nm (2.43 eV) representing σ*(CO) → n(O) and π*(CO) → n(O) electronic transition components, respectively (Fig. S1). The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of the corresponding PL shows the involvement of two distinct photo-excitation centres at 344.8 nm (3.59 eV) and 385.3 nm (3.22 eV) in this photo-physical process representing the electronic transitions π(CC) → ESiSDs and n(O) → ESiSDs, respectively (Fig. S1). Here, ESiSDs represents the intra-band energy states produced from the structural deformation of GQDs by local defects and sp3 hybridization near to sp2 hybridised π-electron networks in GQDs [63–65]. Fig. 3 [A] confirms that the green luminescence of GQDs is actually originated from the absorbance edge in the near-UV region involving the ESiSDs and other energy states of the oxygenated functional groups of GQDs. The PL and PLE of GQDs greatly depend on the pH of the dispersion (Fig. 3 [B], Table S1 and Fig. S2). The normalised PL intensity significantly increases with increasing the pH of the dispersion from 1.6 to 4.1, then remains almost stable in the pH range 4.1–7.0 and finally falls significantly at further higher pH (Fig. 3 [B] and Fig. S3). An additional PL (de-convoluted) peak is observed at 415 nm in dilute acidic dispersion and it is effectively disappeared at pH 4.1 and higher (Fig. S2 and Table S1). The epoxide functional group is stable in dilute acids and it eventually gets converted into hydroxyl group with increasing pH. Therefore, the small PL peak at 415 nm could be assigned as σ*(COC) → n(O) electronic transition originating from the epoxide centers of GQDs (Fig. S1). The drastic decrease in the PL intensities at pH < 4.1 is a combined effect of the static and dynamic quenching of PL of GQDs by H+ ions, where in the one hand, the protonation of the oxygeneted functional groups opens up the channels for non-radiative transition, on the other hand, the π-π* transitions of sp2 carbons are quenched through collision quenching by H+ ions. In contrast, the decrease in the PL intensities of GQDs at pH > 7.0 is attributed to the dynamic (collision) quenching of GQDs by excess OH− ions [64].

The wavelength of the PL maxima does not systematically vary with pH (Fig. S3) and it indicates no involvement of red-giant effect in the GQDs used in this study. The further studies have been performed with the aqueous dispersion of GQDs at pH 5.4. The excitation wavelength dependent PL of GQDs represents the involvement of multi-energy states in the photo-physical process (Fig. 3 [C]). The applied excitation energy fairly overlaps with the sharp PLE peak centered at 345 nm while the excitation wavelengths are scanned from 300 nm to 345 nm and thus, the PL peak intensity is quickly built up at same λmax about 460 nm (Fig. 3 [D & E]). At longer excitation wavelength than 345 nm, the total number of excited electrons decreases, because the extent of overlap between the applied excitation energy and broad exciton hump centered at 385 nm then only governs the intensity of the PL. Then few low energy PLs involving couple of low energy states also get accounted in the overall PL and as a result, the λmax of PL is shifted towards the longer wavelength about 525 nm (Fig. 3 [E]). This explanation is also supported by the trend of PLE intensities at 345 nm as a function of excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3 [E]). The average PL lifetime (τav) of the GQDs at λmax(PL) 460 nm is calculated as 2.9 ns by fitting the experimental TCSPC data with the tri-exponential function (Eq. (1)) (Fig. 3 [F]).

The PL of GQD is attributed to a moderately fast transition having lifetime of 1.70 ns (39%), a very fast transition having lifetime of 0.29 ns (25%) and a delayed transition having lifetime of 6.02 ns (36%).

The measured volumes of aqueous stock solution U (pH 5.7) are successively added into 2 mL aqueous dispersion of GQDs at pH 5.4 and the corresponding PL is recorded at the excitation wavelength of 345 nm (Fig. 4
[A]). The PL of GQDs is decreased by ∼ 42% after addition of 21.43 ppb of U. The plot of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration (in ppb or μg L−1) of U i.e. [U] shows a linear dynamic range (2.24–21.43 ppb) in the concentrations of our interest (Fig. 4 [B]). Here, F0 and F represent the PL intensities (at λmax(PL) = 460 nm) of GQDs in the absence and presence of U, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) for U are calculated as 0.56 ppb and 1.87 ppb, respectively, by using Eqs. (2) & (3).

where, σ and s are the standard deviation in the blank solution and sensitivity of the calibration plot, respectively. Therefore, the sensing and determination of U in a homogeneous dispersion with GQDs are very competitive in the contemporary developments in the field (Table 1 ) [66–76].

The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of 21.43 ppb U (a) in water at pH 5.4 shows the characteristic absorbance of nitrate in the wavelength region 200–350 nm (Fig. 4 [C]) [77]. Further, the U(VI)-aqua species produces some weak spectral absorbance structures in the wavelength range 350–450 nm (Fig. S4) [78]. The absorbance of aqueous dispersion of GQDs at pH 5.4 (b) is significantly modified upon addition of 21.43 ppb U into it (c) (Fig. 4 [C]). The absorbance of GQDs in the UV region (200–300 nm) is enhanced, while the absorption band tail in the wavelength range 400–900 nm is diminished upon addition of uranium at ultra-trace levels. Further, the PLE peaks (for λmax(PL) = 460 nm) centered at 345 nm and 385 nm of GQD is also suppressed by about 40% (Notably, this is the extent of suppression in PLE as similar to the suppression of PL, although the PLE of pure GQDs has slightly reached the limit of saturation of the instrument) in the presence of 21.43 ppb U (Fig. 4 [D]). At pH 5.4, GQDs have negatively charged COO− functional groups [15,79] and uranium exists as positively charged species [80,81]. Therefore, uranium ions electrostatically interact with the defect states and oxygenated functional groups (OH, CO, COO− etc.) of GQDs and their association improves the planarity of GQDs by eliminating some of the structural deformations present in the free GQDs [80,82,83]. Thus, the loss of ESiSDs, which are mainly responsible for the fluorescence of GQDs, upon addition of U actually quenches the PL of GQDs and that is why the addition of U has not altered the PL lifetime of GQDs (Fig. S5 and Table S2). The Stern-Volmer rate constant (KSV) and the bimolecular quenching rate constant (Kq) for the static quenching of PL of GQDs are calculated as 0.03395 ppb−1 (8.1 × 106 M−1) and 1.2 × 107 ppb−1 s−1 (2.8 × 1015 M−1 s−1), respectively (Fig. 4 [E]) by following Eqs. (4) & (5).

where, F0 and F are PL intensities in the absence and presence of U, respectively. The Kq of GQD-U system is found to be very high (in the order of ∼1015 M−1 s−1) because of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the GQDs and U by virtue of a small overlap between the PL of GQDs and absorbance of U in the wavelength range 400–450 nm. The average number of quenching site (n) and the binding affinity (Ka) are calculated from the slope and intercept, respectively, of plot of log[(F0-F)/F0] versus log[U] as shown in Fig. S6. (Eq. (6))

The number of binding sites per PL centre is 1.2 and Ka is calculated as 7.2 × 107 M−1. Here, we are the first to prove that other than the nonspecific electrostatic association of the negatively charged oxygenated functional groups of GQDs and the positively charged metal ions, the resonance energy transfer from the GQDs to the molecular orbitals of the uranium species is also responsible for the quenching of the photoluminescence of the GQDs in the presence of U, although the contribution of the former is minor in the overall PL quenching process. The interference of 10 mM of mono-valent (Na+ & K+), di-valent (Sr2+ & Mg2+) and tri-valent (Al3+ & Fe3+) cations in the quenching process is shown in Fig. 4 [E]. The Na+ & K+ hardly affect the PL of GQDs, but Sr2+ & Mg2+ suppress the PL of GQDs by 29% and 25%, respectively. Although, Al3+ suppresses the PL of GQDs only by 8%, Fe3+ suppresses the PL of GQDs by 98% suggesting a strong interference in the determination of U. Therefore, GQDs prepared by our unconventional synthesis route can be used for sensitive determination of U by PL methodology in a pure solution in the front end of the nuclear technology at lowest LOD ever achieved by any carbon nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalysis of U by GQDs and heterogeneous determination of U at ultra-trace levels
ce:section.ce:para:
Molecular hydrogen has evolved on Au by the reduction of water in sat. Na2CO3 (Eq. (7)) solution at potentials more cathodic to −1.6 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) (Fig. 5
[A](a)).

GQDs are highly dispersible in water and thus the stability of the drop-casted GQDs on general electrode during the electrochemical studies is uncertain. Further, we have already reported that the drop-casted GQDs, which are electrochemically reduced (will be designated henceforth as ERGQDs) in sat. Na2CO3 are impending electrocatalyst [52]. Therefore, the electrochemistry of U is studied on ERGQDs prepared by cyclic voltammetry through reducing 33.2 μg (w) of GQDs drop-casted on Au in sat. Na2CO3 at a scan rate (ν) of 0.05 V s−1 in a single cycle in the potential window 0.1 V to −2.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) (Fig. 5 [A](b)), where 8.4 μmol g−1 (calculated by Eq. (8)) of the carboxyl and carbonyl functional groups of GQDs are reduced to alkoxy groups at −1.42 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) following Eqs. (9)–(11) [52,84–88]:

where, n and q are the amount and integrated charge of the reducible functional groups; z is the average number of exchangeable electrons involved in the reduction reactions of carboxyl and cabonyl functional groups; F is the Faraday’s constant (96485C mol−1).
(9) −COO− + 2e− + 2 H2 O=−C − OH + 3 OH−

The electrochemical performance of the ERGQDs is initially checked by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] + 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) in 0.1 M KCl (pH 6.7) at 0.138 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) (measured as open circuit potential) fixed potential with an amplitude of 0.005 V in the frequency range 1 × 105–1 Hz. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of this reaction is calculated by fitting the Nyquist plots (Fig. 5 [B]) with the Randles’s electrochemical equivalent circuit, Rs(Q[RctW]), where Rs and W represent the solution resistance and Warburg impedance accounting the concentration polarisation, respectively. The [] and () represent the series and parallel connections between the circuit elements. Q (ZQ = 1/(Y0(jω)n), where, 0 ≤ n ≤ 1) is the constant phase element accounting the electrode roughness; Y0 and n are the pre-factor and exponent, respectively. Since, calculated n is close to 1, thus we can ascertain that Q could be a representation of the double-layer capacitance in real condition. It can be seen that (b) ERGQDs, having Rct = 176 Ω, is more electrochemically active compared to (a) Au, having Rct = 231.4 Ω. The other important fitted parameters are listed in Table S3. The phase angle Bode plot also indicates that the double layer capacitance decreases in ERGNO as compared to Au (inset of Fig. 5 [B]).

The CVs of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 (pH 10.9) on (a) Au and (b) ERGQDs at ν = 0.025 V s−1 are shown in Fig. 5 [C]. The CV of (c) sat. Na2CO3 (pH 10.9) on ERGQDs under similar condition is also superimposed in Fig. 5 [C]. The reduction of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− on Au shows a cathodic peak at −0.808 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) (Ep c) having the cathodic peak current density (jp c) −0.39 mA cm−2. In the reverse potential sweep direction, oxidation of [U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− on Au shows an anodic peak at −0.662 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) (Ep a) having the anodic peak current density (jp a) 0.29 mA cm−2. The apparent formal potential (E0′ = 0.5 × (Ep a + Ep c)) and peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp = Ep a − Ep c) are calculated as −0.735 V [85] and 0.146 V, respectively. The reduction of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− on ERGQDs happens at lower over-potentials showing the Ep c at −0.771 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) having jp c −0.44 mA cm−2. In the reverse potential sweep, oxidation of [U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− on ERGQDs also happens at lower over-potentials showing the Ep a at −0.689 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) having jp a 0.36 mA cm−2. The decrease of ΔEp to 0.082 V on ERGQDs indicates the superior heterogeneous electron transfers by ERGNO to [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− and [U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− compared to the metallic gold electrode.

Fig. S7 shows the CVs of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 (pH 10.9) on (a) glassy carbon (GC) and (b) ERGQDs at ν = 0.025 V s−1 for comparing the electrochemical performances of ERGQDs to the common carbon base GC electrode. The electrocatalysis of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4−/[U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− redox reaction in sat. Na2CO3 (pH 10.9) on ERGQDs is also confirmed by the EIS study, where Rct of the reaction decreases from 2052 Ω (on Au) to 538 Ω (on ERGQDs) (Fig. 5 [D] and Table S4). The frequency of highest phase shift (φ), as observed in the Bode Phase angle plot (inset of Fig. 5 [D]), is shifted towards the higher frequency (1322 Hz) on ERGQDs compared to the corresponding frequency (1150 Hz) observed for Au. Further, we have shown that, the COO− functional groups of GQDs have been converted to COH functional groups during the electrochemical reduction of GQDs in sat. Na2CO3 solution (Eq. (9)). Therefore, the electrocatalysis by ERGQDs for the negatively charged redox species [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4−/[U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− in sat. Na2CO3 (pH 10.9) and [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− in 0.1 M KCl (pH 6.7) is believed to be happened through the hydroxyl groups and hole structures of the ERGQDs governed by a diffusion controlled mass-transfer of the analytes towards the electrode [89].

Fig. 6 shows the CVs of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 on [A] Au and [B] ERGQDs at ν = 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250 and 300 mV s−1. The characteristic parameters such as Ep c, Ep a, ΔEp, jp c, jp a, E0′, cathodic half-wave potential (Ec p/2), cathodic transfer coefficient (αc) of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4−/[U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− redox reaction on Au and ERGQDs are listed in Table 2 . ERGQDs (0.72 ± 0.03) show better αc compared to Au (0.55 ± 0.03) for the reduction of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4−. The slopes of the plots of ln(jp c) versus ln(ν) & ln(jp a) versus ln(ν) of this electrochemical reaction are calculated as 0.44 (R2 = 0.9997) & 0.40 (R2 = 0.9928) and 0.48 (R2 = 0.9996) & 0.50 (R2 = 0.9992) for Au and ERGQDs, respectively. It represents the involvement of the diffusion control mass transfer process in the electrochemical reduction of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− and oxidation of [U(V)O2(CO3)3]5− on both Au and ERGQDs. The diffusion coefficient (D) of [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 at room temperature (T = 298 K) is calculated as 8.4 × 10−5 cm2 s−1 from the slope of the plot of jp c versus ν1/2 by following Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (12)) (Fig. S8):

Therefore, αc (Eq. (13)) and Ψ (a dimensionless parameter expressed by Eq. (14) introduced to solve the dimensionless linear integral equation with variable coefficients for a reversible heterogeneous electron transfer reaction) [90] are the possible key parameters to control the shapes of the voltammograms of U(VI)/U(V) on Au and ERGQDs.

where, R and T are the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) and temperature in K, respectively. On increasing ν, the Ep c and Ep a are shifted cathodically and anodically, respectively, resulting into the increase in ΔEp for both Au and ERGQDs (Table 2).

However, the rate of increase of ΔEp with respect to ν is higher in Au compared to ERGQDs. Therefore, it can be expected that the value of Ψ dominates over α in the electrochemistry of U(VI)/U(V) on Au and ERGQDs. The electrochemically reversible redox reactions have Ψ ≥ 7 and then ΔEp becomes independent of Ψ and α. At lower values of Ψ, the redox reactions become electrochemically quasi-reversible and then ΔEp depends on both Ψ and α. Nicholson calculated the ΔEps of theoretically simulated CVs for a range of Ψs assuming α = 0.5 (Table 1 in Reference [90] and similar exercise was performed by Heinze with more data points (Table 2 in Ref. [91]). The data presented in those Tables are used to calculate Ψ from the experimentally observed ΔEp (in V) values for any quasi-reversible redox reaction through two different empirical equations as derived by Ikeda et al. [92] (Eq. (15)) and Lavagnini et al. [93] (Eq. (16a)).

The k0 is calculated from the slope of the plot of Ψ versus [(nπFDν)/RT]−1/2 (assuming α = 0.5) by following Eq. (14). We have fitted the data of Heinze (Fig. S9[A]) and Nicholson (Fig. S9[B]) with the model empirical equations similar to the empirical equations proposed by Ikeda et Al. and Lavagnini et Al. and compared both the models with respect to the merit of the fittings (Table S5). It shows that the correlation between the said empirical equations (Eqs. (15) & (16a)) is not convincing, although the Ψ versus ΔEp for α = 0.5 calculated by (i) Heinze and (ii) Nicholson are graphically correlated (Fig. 6 [C]). On the other hand, those empirical equations are not valid for α ≠ 0.5 and ΔEp ≥ 0.150 V, which are often encountered in the real life experiments. In identical set of conditions except for the variation only in the nature of the working electrodes, this approach had been used for comparison in the cases where ΔEp ≥ 0.150 V [94]. Klingler and Kochi (KK) tried to solve this issue by another way of approach to the quasi-reversible reaction with Eqs. (17) & (18) derived from the Nicholson’s equations related to the irreversible oxidation reaction [54].

Although, the k0 values calculated by KK’s method resembles with the value of the same calculated by the Nicholson’s equation in the ΔEp range 0.150–0.200 V, it has also been reported that k0 calculated by KK’s approach differs significantly (some-times by an order also) from the same calculated by Nicholson’s approach for the identical system [91,95]. Further, it is reported by KK that k0 becomes independent of ΔEp when it exceeds 0.300 V and the reason is unknown [54]. Hence, we believe that the existing approaches for evaluating the comparative kinetics related to the electrocatalysis phenomenon are, at present, very complicated and often being misinterpreted in many literatures. Therefore, in the process of understanding the electrocatalysis of U by ERGQDs in comparison to Au, we have re-evaluated the related developments from the respective roots and have tried to frame a simple, general and convincing approach for evaluating k0 from the cyclic voltammograms. The important Equations (Equations S1–S18), indicating the major steps, are shown in the Appendix SA in the Supporting information File. Starting from the Nicholson’s equations related to both the irreversible oxidation and reduction reactions, we have derived a more general expression for k0 of a quasi-reversible system (Eqs. (19) & (20)):

Insets of Fig. 6 [C] show that 1/Ψ linearly depends on ΔEp, when ΔEp ≤ 0.115 V; but it exponentially depends on ΔEp when ΔEp ≥ 0.125 V. Hence, it can be understood that 1/Ψ versus ΔEp has a conjugation of linear dependency [92,94] and exponential dependency [93,54] depending on the values of ΔEp. Therefore, we find it suitable to use a polynomial equation (covering the feature of both the linear expression and the series expansion of the exponential functions) as a expression for the entire range of ΔEp (Eq. (22)):

Notably, the combination of the first two terms of Eq. (22) is very similar to the empirical equation (Eq. (15)) proposed by Ikeda et al. [92] It is to be noted that, Eq. (22) is not just a series expansion of an exponential function and thus it can explain the reason of the existing differences in the k0 values calculated by KK’s-equations and Nicholson’s-equation at the low ΔEps (Fig. 12 in [54]). Although, we do not completely eliminate the influence of α on the values of the coefficients in the right hand side of Eq. (22) unless doing the detailed investigations of the influence of α and higher values of ΔEp on the coefficients, Eq. (22) is found to be satisfactorily best one among the existing empirical formulas for the purpose. Hence, 1/Ψ is calculated for our experiments from the ΔEp values as reported in Table 2 and Fig. 6 [A, B] by Eq. (22). Then, k0 of the U(VI)/U(V) electrochemical reaction is calculated from the slope of the plot of Ψ versus [(nπFDν)/RT]−1/2. The ERGQDs (1.8 × 10−2 cm s−1) shows higher k0 for U(VI)/U(V) redox reaction compared to bare Au (0.4 × 10−2 cm s−1) because of the catalytic interactions of hydroxyl groups and hole structures of the ERGQDs with the uranium species.

Differential pulse voltammograms (DPV) and square wave voltammogram (SWV) are commonly being used for the electrochemical determination of analyte due to their higher sensitivity compared to CV. The cathodic DPV of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 shows the cathodic peak current density of −0.36 mA cm−2 at −0.735 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (a) Au and −1.02 mA cm−2 at −0.709 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (b) ERGQDs (Fig. 7
[A]). On the other hand, the anodic DPV of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]5− in sat. Na2CO3 shows the anodic peak current density of 0.41 mA cm−2 at −0.739 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (a′) Au and 1.02 mA cm−2 at −0.746 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (b′) ERGQDs (Fig. 7 [A]). The higher cathodic and anodic current densities as well as the lower cathodic and anodic overpotentials of U(VI)/U(V) redox reaction on ERGQDs compared to Au are attributed to the electrocatalysis phenomenon as discussed in the earlier paragraphs.

The cathodic SWV of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 shows the cathodic peak current density of −1.85 mA cm−2 at −0.745 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (a) Au and −4.17 mA cm−2 at −0.733 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (b) ERGQDs (Fig. 7 [B]). On the other hand, the anodic SWV of 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]5− in sat. Na2CO3 shows the anodic peak current density of 2.27 mA cm−2 at −0.724 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (a′) Au and 4.42 mA cm−2 at −0.727 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) on (b′) ERGQDs (Fig. 7 [B]). The cathodic SWV is found to be much superior compared to the cathodic DPV and thus we have utilised the SWV technique to determine U at the ultra-trace levels. Fig. 7 [C] shows the progressive increase of the cathodic SWV peak current with increasing the U concentration in the range 23.4–681.5 ppb in sat. Na2CO3. The mean peak current densities at (i) Au and (ii) ERGQDs along with the standard deviation for eight numbers of repeatable measurements for each concentration of U(VI) in the solution are plotted in Fig. 7 [D]. The linear dynamic ranges (LDR) of the calibration plot on Au and ERGQDs are found up 23.4-116.4 ppb and 23.4-345.8 ppb, respectively. The increase in the population of the electrochemically active sites on ERGQDs has elongated the LDR to higher concentrations of U. The sensitivity of ERGQDs is calculated as 1.11 μA cm−2 ppb−1, which is much higher compared to that of Au (0.83 μA cm−2 ppb−1) as evident from Fig. 7 [D]. The LOD and LOQ of U on ERGQDs are calculated as 2 and 6.7 ppb, respectively, by using the equivalent expressions of Eqs. (2) & (3). The result is not only superior to the calculated LOD (9.2 ppb) and LOQ (30.7 ppb) of U on Au, but also competitive to the existing electrochemical methodologies for the same purpose (Table 1).

We have shown that, the LOD (2 ppb) for the electrochemical determination of U by GQDs is little higher compared to the LOD (0.56 ppb) of the determination of the same by fluorescence quenching methods, but the former has the additional advantage over the later in its applicability in the aqueous solutions having high ionic strengths and interfering ions like Fe3+ etc. which are mostly precipitated out in sat. Na2CO3. When required amount of aqueous stock solution of FeCl3 was added into sat. Na2CO3 to prepare 0.8 mM solution of Fe(III) in sat. Na2CO3, then red Fe(OH)3 is instantly precipitated out and that precipitate is not disappeared even after ultrasonication for 30 min. We have allowed the red precipitate to settle down overnight and taken the supernatant liquid (having unknown amount of fine colloidal particles of Fe(OH)3) for further electrochemical experiments. Fig. S10 shows the cathodic SWVs on ERGQDs in (1) sat. Na2CO3 (black line), (2) 0.8 mM U(VI) in sat. Na2CO3 (red line), (3) colloidal dispersion of Fe(OH)3 in sat. Na2CO3 (red line) and mixed solution of 0.8 mM U(VI) and colloidal dispersion of Fe(OH)3 in sat. Na2CO3 (magenta line). The colloidal dispersion of Fe(OH)3 in sat. Na2CO3 shows a broad cathodic peak in the potential range 0 to −0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl(sat. KCl) [96] and notably the colour of the modified electrode is also changed to red probably due to the physical adsorption of the fine colloidal Fe(III)/Fe(II) particles on the ERGNO modified electrodes. In the mixed solution of U(VI) and colloidal suspension of Fe(OH)3 in sat. Na2CO3, the cathodic peak of U electrocatalysed further by either the catalytic action of the adsorbed Fe(III)/Fe(II) colloids adsorbed on the ERGNO or by a coupled chemical reaction between Fe(III)/Fe(II) and U(VI)/U(V) redox couples [97,98]. This observation is encouraging and the results of the detailed investigations with greater insights will be communicated separately in the follow-up articles.

The recovery test with 10 and 15 ppb of U, as presented in Table S6, confirms the absence of any systematic bias in the procedure. Fig. 7 [E] shows the electrochemical stability of ERGQDs in the U solution. No distortion of CV and no decrease in the peak current are observed upon consecutive 30 potential cycles of ERGQDs in 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 at ν = 0.05 V s−1. The electrodes modified with ERGQDs are very reproducible (Fig. 7 [F]). The four electrodes (E1, E2, E3, E4) prepared by following the same procedure show almost similar CVs in 0.8 mM [U(VI)O2(CO3)3]4− in sat. Na2CO3 at ν = 0.025 V s−1. The jp c of the cathodic SWV of four electrodes varied within 4.6% (Inset of Fig. 7 [F]), which is of course not originated from the random errors in electrochemical analysis (statistically evaluated by one-way ANOVA test of ip c as ANOVA (F(3,36) = 585.7, p = 1.4 × 10−30), Table S7), rather a methodological error generates from the variation in the amount of the ERGQDs remained on the base electrode after the reduction of GQDs (Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the sensing performance in real ground water sample
ce:section.ce:para:
The ground water (pH 7.5) collected from the lake in the Punjab state is found by ICP-MS to be consisted with mostly Mg (<100 μg/mL), U (∼26 ng/mL), Sr (∼1.2 μg/mL), Ti (∼0.2 μg/mL), Mo (∼0.2 μg/mL), W (∼0.2 μg/mL) and Cr, Mn, Cu, Zn, Y, V each at levels less than 0.1 μg/mL. The water is centrifuged with an ultracentrifuge (Sigma, model no. 3K30) and 2 mL ground water is mixed with 4 mL of sat. Na2CO3 for the cathodic SWV analysis. The pH of the mixture is measured as 10.55. The cathodic SWV of that mixture solution is recorded (number of repetitive measurements = 6) on ERGQDs under optimized condition as described previously (Fig. 8
(i)). Then the cathodic SWV is recorded after adding known concentrations of U(VI) into that mixture (number of repetitive measurements = 6) (Fig. 8 (ii–vi)). The cathodic peak current (Ip c) is measured from the baseline of the peak for each cathodic SWV. The concentration of U in the electrochemical cell is calculated as 8.9 ppb from the intercept of the straight line (red line) at the x-axis in the standard addition plot (Inset of Fig. 8). Therefore, the concentration of U in the ground water is calculated by the cathodic SWV on ERGQDs as 26.7 ppb, which is validated by the measured value (26 ppb) by ICP-MS. Therefore, we can confirm that GQDs are suitable for sensing U at the ultra-trace levels in the ground water.

We have successfully demonstrated a novel and unconventional route to prepare GQDs at room temperature through a chemically clean, single-step, simultaneous oxido-reductive, non-contact process. The GQDs prepared here have shown the coexisting photoluminescence and electrocatalytic superiorities. Hence, the GQDs have been utilized for the first time to develop the complimentary dual purpose sensors suitable for the determination of U at ultra-trace levels in pure nuclear fuel samples and the drinking water. Here, we have showed that the electrostatic association of U with the oxygenated functional groups of GQDs followed by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from GQDs to U quenches the photoluminescence of the homogeneous aqueous dispersion of GQDs in the presence of U. Further, the inherent electrocatalytic activity of the as prepared GQDs becomes successful to remove the existing incompatibility barrier in using GQDs for the inner- and outer- sphere electron transfer reactions. Moreover, the catalytic electron transfer reaction between the casted GQDs (or more specifically the electrochemically reduced casted GQDs on gold electrode) and the U species present in water having high ionic strength enhances the sensitivity and thus lowers the LOD for the determination of U down to the ultra-trace levels compared to noble metal such as gold. Apart from the utilization of the dual-excellences of the GQDs for developing U sensors; here we have also stepped ahead for removing the existing perplexities in choosing the suitable kinetic equations (such as Nicholson or Klingler-Kochi equations) for studying the electrode kinetics of the electrocatalytic reactions derived from the experimentally observed peak-to-peak separations in the CVs at different potential scan rates. Here, we have developed a much simpler and general kinetic emperical equation, which is suitable to study the electrode kinetics of the electrocatalytic reactions irrespective of the values of the peak-to-peak separations in the CVs. This is indeed a priliminary step and thus invites fresh looks into this topic. This approach keeps the hopes to simplify, and more importantly unify the approaches for studying the electrodics in electrocalalysis, electrochemical sensors, fuel cells, photoelectrochemistry etc. Hence, the developments presented in this article would not only pave the unconventional, easy and environmentally clean way to prepare GQDs possessing both photochemical and electrochemical excellences expected from a sustainable real-life adaptable sensor; but also led to simultaneous and multipurpose usages of GQDs in various industrial and environmental applications.
Authors declare no conflicts of interest.

We sincerely thank Dr. Hirendra N. Ghosh and Mr. Jayanta Dana from RPCD for providing us the facilities for the photophysical studies. We acknowledge Dr. R. S. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Saurav K. Guin is Scientific Officer in Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Department of Atomic Energy, India. He joined BARC in 2007 after graduating from 50th Batch of BARC Training School. He obtained M.Sc. degree in 2006 in Chemistry from the University of Calcutta in Kolkata. He received PhD degree from the Homi Bhabha National Institute in 2015 through his thesis on “Template-free electrosynthesis of metal nanoparticles”. His research work includes electrochemical synthesis of modified electrodes with metal nanoparticles, conducting polymers, carbon nanomaterials for electrocatalysis and electrochemical sensors. He also works on the theoretical and computational electrochemistry. He has received best presentation awards in the various international conferences in Mexico, United Kingdom and India. He is a regular reviewer of several international peer-reviewed journals. He was the Secretary of the Indian Society for ElectroAnalytical Chemistry (ISEAC) for consecutive two terms (2010–2016) and organised international conferences. He is member of the International Society of Electrochemistry (ISE) and Indian Association of Nuclear Chemists and Allied Scientists (IANCAS).

Mr. Arvind S. Ambolikar is Technical Officer in Fuel Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Department of Atomic Energy, India. He joined BARC in 2008 after graduating from R.T.M. Nagpur University. He is working on synthesis, characterization and electrochemical applications of modified electrodes for actinides. He is pursuing PhD in the Homi Bhabha National Institute. He was the treasurer of the Indian Society for ElectroAnalytical Chemistry (ISEAC) for one term (2013–2016).

Dr. Jhimli Paul Guin is Scientific Officer in Radiation Technology Development Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Department of Atomic Energy, India. She joined BARC in 2008 after graduating from 51st Batch of BARC Training School. She obtained M.Sc. degree in 2006 in Chemistry from the University of Calcutta in Kolkata. She received PhD degree from the Homi Bhabha National Institute in 2015 through her thesis on “Effects of Radiation on Waste Water Containing Organic Pollutants”. She is expert in the synthesis of polymer grafted synthetic and bioadsorbents for removal of pollutants, several hydrogels and thermostable elastic gels with metal nanoparticles, polymer composite with metal nanoparticles and carbon nanomaterials for sensor, catalysis and carriers for drug delivery applications. Her research interest also includes the advanced oxidation processes including photocatalysis, ozonolysis, gamma radiolysis and electron beam treatment for the efficient decolouration and mineralization of highly coloured and heavy organic content loaded textile effluents, emerging organic pollutants and endocrine disrupting compounds to make water clean and reusable. She is expert in the synthesis of advanced molecularly imprinted polymers for sensing endocrine disrupting compounds and inorganic hazardous chemicals in water. She received several awards in the national and international conferences in India. She is a regular reviewer of several international peer-reviewed journals.

Dr. Suman Neogy joined Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai in 2001 after graduating in Metallurgical Engineering from Jadavpur University, Kolkata. Since then he has been working as Scientific Officer and is presently holding the designation of Scientific Officer (G). He has obtained his Ph.D. degree from Homi Bhabha National Institute, Mumbai. His specialization includes physical metallurgy and phase transformation studies in metallic systems and transmission electron microscopy.
